title: Surrealism

infobox.value: [Breton](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Breton "André Breton"), [Carrington](/wiki/Leonora_Carrington "Leonora Carrington"), [Dalí](/wiki/Salvador_Dal%C3%AD "Salvador Dalí"), [Ernst](/wiki/Max_Ernst "Max Ernst"), [Fini](/wiki/Leonor_Fini "Leonor Fini"), [Magritte](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9_Magritte "René Magritte"), [Oppenheim](/wiki/M%C3%A9ret_Oppenheim "Méret Oppenheim")

| * [Surrealist Manifesto](/wiki/Surrealist_Manifesto "Surrealist Manifesto") * [Afro-Surrealism](/wiki/Afro-Surrealism "Afro-Surrealism") * [Birmingham Surrealists](/wiki/Birmingham_Surrealists "Birmingham Surrealists") * [Women Surrealists](/wiki/Women_Surrealists "Women Surrealists") * [New Primitivism](/wiki/New_Primitivism "New Primitivism")

is an [art](/wiki/Art_movement "Art movement") and [cultural movement](/wiki/Cultural_movement "Cultural movement") that developed in Europe in the [aftermath of World War I](/wiki/Aftermath_of_World_War_I "Aftermath of World War I") in which artists aimed to allow the [unconscious mind](/wiki/Unconscious_mind "Unconscious mind") to express itself, often resulting in the depiction of [illogical](/wiki/Illogical "Illogical") or [dreamlike](/wiki/Dreamlike "Dreamlike") scenes and ideas.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Its intention was, according to leader [André Breton](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Breton "André Breton"), to "resolve the previously contradictory conditions of dream and reality into an absolute reality, a super-reality", or *surreality.*[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Manifesto_of_Surrealism-4) It produced works of painting, writing, photography, [theatre](/wiki/Theatre_of_Cruelty "Theatre of Cruelty"), [filmmaking](/wiki/Surrealist_cinema "Surrealist cinema"), [music](/wiki/Surrealist_music "Surrealist music"), [comedy](/wiki/Surreal_humour "Surreal humour") and other media as well.

**Surrealism** is an [art](/wiki/Art_movement "Art movement") and [cultural movement](/wiki/Cultural_movement "Cultural movement") that developed in Europe in the [aftermath of World War I](/wiki/Aftermath_of_World_War_I "Aftermath of World War I") in which artists aimed to allow the [unconscious mind](/wiki/Unconscious_mind "Unconscious mind") to express itself, often resulting in the depiction of [illogical](/wiki/Illogical "Illogical") or [dreamlike](/wiki/Dreamlike "Dreamlike") scenes and ideas.[[1]](#cite_note-1) Its intention was, according to leader [André Breton](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Breton "André Breton"), to "resolve the previously contradictory conditions of dream and reality into an absolute reality, a super-reality", or *surreality.*[[2]](#cite_note-2)[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-Manifesto_of_Surrealism-4) It produced works of painting, writing, photography, [theatre](/wiki/Theatre_of_Cruelty "Theatre of Cruelty"), [filmmaking](/wiki/Surrealist_cinema "Surrealist cinema"), [music](/wiki/Surrealist_music "Surrealist music"), [comedy](/wiki/Surreal_humour "Surreal humour") and other media as well.

Works of Surrealism feature the [element of surprise](/wiki/Element_of_surprise "Element of surprise"), unexpected [juxtapositions](/wiki/Juxtaposition "Juxtaposition") and *[non sequitur](/wiki/Non_sequitur_(literary_device) "Non sequitur (literary device)")*. However, many Surrealist artists and writers regard their work as an expression of the philosophical movement first and foremost (for instance, of the "pure psychic [automatism](/wiki/Automatic_behavior "Automatic behavior")" Breton speaks of in the first [Surrealist Manifesto](/wiki/Surrealist_Manifesto "Surrealist Manifesto")), with the works themselves being secondary, i.e., artifacts of surrealist experimentation.[[5]](#cite_note-5) Leader Breton was explicit in his assertion that Surrealism was, above all, a revolutionary movement. At the time, the movement was associated with political causes such as [communism](/wiki/Communism "Communism") and [anarchism](/wiki/Anarchism "Anarchism"). It was influenced by the [Dada](/wiki/Dada "Dada") movement of the 1910s.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

The term "Surrealism" originated with [Guillaume Apollinaire](/wiki/Guillaume_Apollinaire "Guillaume Apollinaire") in 1917.[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8) However, the Surrealist movement was not officially established until after October 1924, when the Surrealist Manifesto published by Breton succeeded in claiming the term for his group over a rival faction led by [Yvan Goll](/wiki/Yvan_Goll "Yvan Goll"), who had published his own surrealist manifesto two weeks prior.[[9]](#cite_note-9) The most important center of the movement was [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris"), France. From the 1920s onward, the movement spread around the globe, impacting the visual arts, literature, theatre, film, and music of many countries and languages, as well as political thought and practice, philosophy, and social and cultural theories.

## Founding of the movement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Surrealism&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Founding of the movement")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/2/2b/The_Elephant_Celebes.jpg/250px-The_Elephant_Celebes.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Elephant_Celebes.jpg)

[Max Ernst](/wiki/Max_Ernst "Max Ernst"), *The Elephant Celebes*, 1921

The word *surrealism* was first coined in March 1917 by [Guillaume Apollinaire](/wiki/Guillaume_Apollinaire "Guillaume Apollinaire").[[10]](#cite_note-10) He wrote in a letter to [Paul Dermée](/wiki/Paul_Derm%C3%A9e "Paul Dermée"): "All things considered, I think in fact it is better to adopt surrealism than supernaturalism, which I first used" [*Tout bien examiné, je crois en effet qu'il vaut mieux adopter surréalisme que surnaturalisme que j'avais d'abord employé*].[[11]](#cite_note-11)

Apollinaire used the term in his program notes for [Sergei Diaghilev](/wiki/Sergei_Diaghilev "Sergei Diaghilev")'s [Ballets Russes](/wiki/Ballets_Russes "Ballets Russes"), *[Parade](/wiki/Parade_(ballet) "Parade (ballet)")*, which premiered 18 May 1917. *Parade* had a one-act scenario by [Jean Cocteau](/wiki/Jean_Cocteau "Jean Cocteau") and was performed with music by [Erik Satie](/wiki/Erik_Satie "Erik Satie"). Cocteau described the ballet as "realistic". Apollinaire went further, describing *Parade* as "surrealistic":[[12]](#cite_note-12)

> This new alliance—I say new, because until now scenery and costumes were linked only by factitious bonds—has given rise, in *Parade*, to a kind of surrealism, which I consider to be the point of departure for a whole series of manifestations of the New Spirit that is making itself felt today and that will certainly appeal to our best minds. We may expect it to bring about profound changes in our arts and manners through universal joyfulness, for it is only natural, after all, that they keep pace with scientific and industrial progress. (Apollinaire, 1917)[[13]](#cite_note-13)

The term was taken up again by Apollinaire, both as subtitle and in the preface to his play *[Les Mamelles de Tirésias: Drame surréaliste](/wiki/The_Breasts_of_Tiresias "The Breasts of Tiresias")*,[[14]](#cite_note-14) which was written in 1903 and first performed in 1917.[[15]](#cite_note-15)

[World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") scattered the writers and artists who had been based in Paris, and in the interim, many became involved with Dada, believing that excessive rational thought and [bourgeois](/wiki/Bourgeois "Bourgeois") values had brought the conflict of the war upon the world. The Dadaists protested with [anti-art](/wiki/Anti-art "Anti-art") gatherings, performances, writings and art works. After the war, when they returned to Paris, the Dada activities continued.

During the war, [André Breton](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Breton "André Breton"), who had trained in medicine and psychiatry, served in a [neurological](/wiki/Neurology "Neurology") hospital where he used [Sigmund Freud](/wiki/Sigmund_Freud "Sigmund Freud")'s psychoanalytic methods with soldiers suffering from [shell-shock](/wiki/Shell-shock "Shell-shock"). Meeting the young writer [Jacques Vaché](/wiki/Jacques_Vach%C3%A9 "Jacques Vaché"), Breton felt that Vaché was the spiritual son of writer and [pataphysics](/wiki/Pataphysics "Pataphysics") founder [Alfred Jarry](/wiki/Alfred_Jarry "Alfred Jarry"). He admired the young writer's anti-social attitude and disdain for established artistic tradition. Later Breton wrote, "In literature, I was successively taken with [Rimbaud](/wiki/Arthur_Rimbaud "Arthur Rimbaud"), with Jarry, with Apollinaire, with [Nouveau](/wiki/Germain_Nouveau "Germain Nouveau"), with [Lautréamont](/wiki/Comte_de_Lautr%C3%A9amont "Comte de Lautréamont"), but it is Jacques Vaché to whom I owe the most."[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Back in Paris, Breton joined in Dada activities and started the literary journal *[Littérature](/wiki/Litt%C3%A9rature_(magazine) "Littérature (magazine)")* along with [Louis Aragon](/wiki/Louis_Aragon "Louis Aragon") and [Philippe Soupault](/wiki/Philippe_Soupault "Philippe Soupault"). They began experimenting with [automatic writing](/wiki/Surrealist_automatism "Surrealist automatism")—spontaneously writing without censoring their thoughts—and published the writings, as well as accounts of dreams, in the magazine. Breton and Soupault continued writing evolving their techniques of automatism and published *[The Magnetic Fields](/wiki/Les_Champs_Magn%C3%A9tiques "Les Champs Magnétiques")* (1920).

By October 1924, two rival Surrealist groups had formed to publish a [Surrealist Manifesto](/wiki/Surrealist_Manifesto "Surrealist Manifesto"). Each claimed to be successors of a revolution launched by Appolinaire. One group, led by [Yvan Goll](/wiki/Yvan_Goll "Yvan Goll") consisted of [Pierre Albert-Birot](/wiki/Pierre_Albert-Birot "Pierre Albert-Birot"), [Paul Dermée](/wiki/Paul_Derm%C3%A9e "Paul Dermée"), [Céline Arnauld](/wiki/C%C3%A9line_Arnauld "Céline Arnauld"), [Francis Picabia](/wiki/Francis_Picabia "Francis Picabia"), [Tristan Tzara](/wiki/Tristan_Tzara "Tristan Tzara"), [Giuseppe Ungaretti](/wiki/Giuseppe_Ungaretti "Giuseppe Ungaretti"), [Pierre Reverdy](/wiki/Pierre_Reverdy "Pierre Reverdy"), [Marcel Arland](/wiki/Marcel_Arland "Marcel Arland"), [Joseph Delteil](/wiki/Joseph_Delteil "Joseph Delteil"), [Jean Painlevé](/wiki/Jean_Painlev%C3%A9 "Jean Painlevé") and [Robert Delaunay](/wiki/Robert_Delaunay "Robert Delaunay"), among others.[[17]](#cite_note-17) The group led by André Breton claimed that [automatism](/wiki/Surrealist_automatism "Surrealist automatism") was a better tactic for societal change than those of Dada, as led by Tzara, who was now among their rivals. Breton's group grew to include writers and artists from various [media](/wiki/Media_(arts) "Media (arts)") such as [Paul Éluard](/wiki/Paul_%C3%89luard "Paul Éluard"), [Benjamin Péret](/wiki/Benjamin_P%C3%A9ret "Benjamin Péret"), [René Crevel](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9_Crevel "René Crevel"), [Robert Desnos](/wiki/Robert_Desnos "Robert Desnos"), [Jacques Baron](/wiki/Jacques_Baron "Jacques Baron"), [Max Morise](/wiki/Max_Morise "Max Morise"),[[18]](#cite_note-Diary_of_a_Genius-18) [Pierre Naville](/wiki/Pierre_Naville "Pierre Naville"), [Roger Vitrac](/wiki/Roger_Vitrac "Roger Vitrac"), [Gala Éluard](/wiki/Gala_%C3%89luard "Gala Éluard"), [Max Ernst](/wiki/Max_Ernst "Max Ernst"), [Salvador Dalí](/wiki/Salvador_Dal%C3%AD "Salvador Dalí"), [Luis Buñuel](/wiki/Luis_Bu%C3%B1uel "Luis Buñuel"), [Man Ray](/wiki/Man_Ray "Man Ray"), [Hans Arp](/wiki/Hans_Arp "Hans Arp"), [Georges Malkine](/wiki/Georges_Malkine "Georges Malkine"), [Michel Leiris](/wiki/Michel_Leiris "Michel Leiris"), [Georges Limbour](/wiki/Georges_Limbour "Georges Limbour"), [Antonin Artaud](/wiki/Antonin_Artaud "Antonin Artaud"), [Raymond Queneau](/wiki/Raymond_Queneau "Raymond Queneau"), [André Masson](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Masson "André Masson"), [Joan Miró](/wiki/Joan_Mir%C3%B3 "Joan Miró"), [Marcel Duchamp](/wiki/Marcel_Duchamp "Marcel Duchamp"), [Jacques Prévert](/wiki/Jacques_Pr%C3%A9vert "Jacques Prévert"), and [Yves Tanguy](/wiki/Yves_Tanguy "Yves Tanguy"), [Dora Maar](/wiki/Dora_Maar "Dora Maar")[[19]](#cite_note-grove-19)[[20]](#cite_note-sadoul_mon_ami_bunuel-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/d/de/La_Revolution_Surrealiste_cover.jpg)](/wiki/File:La_Revolution_Surrealiste_cover.jpg)

Cover of the first issue of *[La Révolution surréaliste](/wiki/La_R%C3%A9volution_surr%C3%A9aliste "La Révolution surréaliste")*, December 1924

As they developed their philosophy, they believed that Surrealism would advocate the idea that ordinary and depictive expressions are vital and important, but that the sense of their arrangement must be open to the full range of imagination according to the [Hegelian Dialectic](/wiki/Hegelian_Dialectics "Hegelian Dialectics"). They also looked to the [Marxist dialectic](/wiki/Dialectical#Marxist_dialectic "Dialectical") and the work of such theorists as [Walter Benjamin](/wiki/Walter_Benjamin "Walter Benjamin") and [Herbert Marcuse](/wiki/Herbert_Marcuse "Herbert Marcuse").[[22]](#cite_note-22)

Freud's concepts of free association, dream analysis and accessing the unconscious were of crucial importance to the Surrealists in advancing their project to liberate the imagination. They embraced [idiosyncrasy](/wiki/Idiosyncrasy "Idiosyncrasy"), while rejecting the idea of an underlying madness. As Dalí later proclaimed, "There is only one difference between a madman and me. I am not mad."[[18]](#cite_note-Diary_of_a_Genius-18)

Beside the use of dream analysis, they emphasized that "one could combine inside the same frame, elements not normally found together to produce illogical and startling effects."[[23]](#cite_note-PynchonSurralism-23) Breton included the idea of the startling juxtapositions in his 1924 manifesto, taking it in turn from a 1918 essay by poet [Pierre Reverdy](/wiki/Pierre_Reverdy "Pierre Reverdy"), which said: "a juxtaposition of two more or less distant realities. The more the relationship between the two juxtaposed realities is distant and true, the stronger the image will be−the greater its emotional power and poetic reality."[[24]](#cite_note-Breton_(1924)-24)

The group aimed to revolutionize human experience, in its personal, cultural, social, and political aspects. They wanted to free people from false rationality, and restrictive customs and structures. Breton proclaimed that the true aim of Surrealism was "long live the social revolution, and it alone!" To this goal, at various times Surrealists aligned with [communism](/wiki/Communism "Communism") and [anarchism](/wiki/Anarchism "Anarchism").

In 1924, two Surrealist factions declared their philosophy in two separate Surrealist Manifestos. That same year the [Bureau of Surrealist Research](/wiki/Bureau_of_Surrealist_Research "Bureau of Surrealist Research") was established and began publishing the journal *[La Révolution surréaliste](/wiki/La_R%C3%A9volution_surr%C3%A9aliste "La Révolution surréaliste")*.

### Surrealist Manifestos

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Surrealism&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Surrealist Manifestos")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/Yvan_Goll%2C_Surr%C3%A9alisme%2C_Manifeste_du_surr%C3%A9alisme%2C_Volume_1%2C_Number_1%2C_October_1%2C_1924%2C_cover_by_Robert_Delaunay.jpg/250px-Yvan_Goll%2C_Surr%C3%A9alisme%2C_Manifeste_du_surr%C3%A9alisme%2C_Volume_1%2C_Number_1%2C_October_1%2C_1924%2C_cover_by_Robert_Delaunay.jpg)](/wiki/File:Yvan_Goll,_Surr%C3%A9alisme,_Manifeste_du_surr%C3%A9alisme,_Volume_1,_Number_1,_October_1,_1924,_cover_by_Robert_Delaunay.jpg)

[Yvan Goll](/wiki/Yvan_Goll "Yvan Goll"), *Surréalisme*, *Manifeste du surréalisme*,[[25]](#cite_note-Surréalisme-25) Volume 1, Number 1, October 1, 1924, cover by [Robert Delaunay](/wiki/Robert_Delaunay "Robert Delaunay")

Main article: [Surrealist Manifesto](/wiki/Surrealist_Manifesto "Surrealist Manifesto")

Leading up to 1924, two rival surrealist groups had formed. Each group claimed to be successors of a revolution launched by Apollinaire. One group, led by [Yvan Goll](/wiki/Yvan_Goll "Yvan Goll"), consisted of [Pierre Albert-Birot](/wiki/Pierre_Albert-Birot "Pierre Albert-Birot"), [Paul Dermée](/wiki/Paul_Derm%C3%A9e "Paul Dermée"), [Céline Arnauld](/wiki/C%C3%A9line_Arnauld "Céline Arnauld"), [Francis Picabia](/wiki/Francis_Picabia "Francis Picabia"), [Tristan Tzara](/wiki/Tristan_Tzara "Tristan Tzara"), [Giuseppe Ungaretti](/wiki/Giuseppe_Ungaretti "Giuseppe Ungaretti"), [Pierre Reverdy](/wiki/Pierre_Reverdy "Pierre Reverdy"), [Marcel Arland](/wiki/Marcel_Arland "Marcel Arland"), [Joseph Delteil](/wiki/Joseph_Delteil "Joseph Delteil"), [Jean Painlevé](/wiki/Jean_Painlev%C3%A9 "Jean Painlevé") and [Robert Delaunay](/wiki/Robert_Delaunay "Robert Delaunay"), among others.[[26]](#cite_note-Durozoi-26)

The other group, led by Breton, included Aragon, Desnos, Éluard, Baron, Crevel, Malkine, [Jacques-André Boiffard](/wiki/Jacques-Andr%C3%A9_Boiffard "Jacques-André Boiffard") and Jean Carrive, among others.[[27]](#cite_note-27)

Yvan Goll published the *Manifeste du surréalisme*, 1 October 1924, in his first and only issue of *Surréalisme*[[25]](#cite_note-Surréalisme-25) two weeks prior to the release of Breton's *Manifeste du surréalisme*, published by Éditions du Sagittaire, 15 October 1924.

Goll and Breton clashed openly, at one point literally fighting, at the Comédie des Champs-Élysées,[[26]](#cite_note-Durozoi-26) over the rights to the term Surrealism. In the end, Breton won the battle through tactical and numerical superiority.[[28]](#cite_note-28)[[29]](#cite_note-29) Though the quarrel over the anteriority of Surrealism concluded with the victory of Breton, the history of surrealism from that moment would remain marked by fractures, resignations, and resounding excommunications, with each surrealist having their own view of the issue and goals, and accepting more or less the definitions laid out by André Breton.[[30]](#cite_note-30)[[31]](#cite_note-31)

Breton's 1924 *Surrealist Manifesto* defines the purposes of Surrealism. He included citations of the influences on Surrealism, examples of Surrealist works, and discussion of Surrealist automatism. He provided the following definitions:

> **Dictionary:** Surrealism, n. Pure psychic automatism, by which one proposes to express, either verbally, in writing, or by any other manner, the real functioning of thought. Dictation of thought in the absence of all control exercised by reason, outside of all aesthetic and moral preoccupation.  
>   
> **Encyclopedia:** Surrealism. Philosophy. Surrealism is based on the belief in the superior reality of certain forms of previously neglected associations, in the omnipotence of dream, in the disinterested play of thought. It tends to ruin once and for all other psychic mechanisms and to substitute itself for them in solving all the principal problems of life.[[4]](#cite_note-Manifesto_of_Surrealism-4)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/1/10/%27Woman_with_Her_Throat_Cut%27%2C_a_flor_piece_by_Giacometti%2C_1932_%28cast_1949%29%2C_Museum_of_Modern_Art%2C_%28New_York_City%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:%27Woman_with_Her_Throat_Cut%27,_a_flor_piece_by_Giacometti,_1932_(cast_1949),_Museum_of_Modern_Art,_(New_York_City).jpg)

[Giacometti](/wiki/Alberto_Giacometti "Alberto Giacometti")'s *Woman with Her Throat Cut*, 1932 (cast 1949), [Museum of Modern Art, New York City](/wiki/Museum_of_Modern_Art "Museum of Modern Art")

The movement in the mid-1920s was characterized by meetings in cafes where the Surrealists played collaborative drawing games, discussed the theories of Surrealism, and developed a variety of [techniques](/wiki/Surrealist_techniques "Surrealist techniques") such as [automatic drawing](/wiki/Surrealist_automatism#Automatic_drawing_and_painting "Surrealist automatism"). Breton initially doubted that visual arts could even be useful in the Surrealist movement since they appeared to be less malleable and open to chance and automatism. This caution was overcome by the discovery of such techniques as [frottage](/wiki/Frottage_(art) "Frottage (art)"), [grattage](/wiki/Grattage "Grattage")[[32]](#cite_note-32) and [decalcomania](/wiki/Decalcomania "Decalcomania").

Soon more visual artists became involved, including [Giorgio de Chirico](/wiki/Giorgio_de_Chirico "Giorgio de Chirico"), [Max Ernst](/wiki/Max_Ernst "Max Ernst"), [Joan Miró](/wiki/Joan_Mir%C3%B3 "Joan Miró"), [Francis Picabia](/wiki/Francis_Picabia "Francis Picabia"), [Yves Tanguy](/wiki/Yves_Tanguy "Yves Tanguy"), [Salvador Dalí](/wiki/Salvador_Dal%C3%AD "Salvador Dalí"), [Luis Buñuel](/wiki/Luis_Bu%C3%B1uel "Luis Buñuel"), [Alberto Giacometti](/wiki/Alberto_Giacometti "Alberto Giacometti"), [Valentine Hugo](/wiki/Valentine_Hugo "Valentine Hugo"), [Méret Oppenheim](/wiki/M%C3%A9ret_Oppenheim "Méret Oppenheim"), [Toyen](/wiki/Toyen "Toyen"), and [Kansuke Yamamoto](/wiki/Kansuke_Yamamoto_(artist) "Kansuke Yamamoto (artist)"). Later, after the [second World War](/wiki/Second_World_War "Second World War"), [Enrico Donati](/wiki/Enrico_Donati "Enrico Donati"), Vinicius Pradella and Denis Fabbri became involved as well. Though Breton admired [Pablo Picasso](/wiki/Pablo_Picasso "Pablo Picasso") and [Marcel Duchamp](/wiki/Marcel_Duchamp "Marcel Duchamp") and courted them to join the movement, they remained peripheral.[[33]](#cite_note-tomkins-33) More writers also joined, including former Dadaist [Tristan Tzara](/wiki/Tristan_Tzara "Tristan Tzara"), [René Char](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9_Char "René Char"), and [Georges Sadoul](/wiki/Georges_Sadoul "Georges Sadoul").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/7/7d/Masson_automatic_drawing.jpg/250px-Masson_automatic_drawing.jpg)](/wiki/File:Masson_automatic_drawing.jpg)

[André Masson](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Masson "André Masson"). Automatic Drawing. 1924. Ink on paper, 23.5 × 20.6 cm. [Museum of Modern Art](/wiki/Museum_of_Modern_Art "Museum of Modern Art"), New York.

In 1925 an autonomous Surrealist group formed in Brussels. The group included the musician, poet, and artist [E. L. T. Mesens](/wiki/E._L._T._Mesens "E. L. T. Mesens"), painter and writer [René Magritte](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9_Magritte "René Magritte"), [Paul Nougé](/wiki/Paul_Noug%C3%A9 "Paul Nougé"), [Marcel Lecomte](/wiki/Marcel_Lecomte "Marcel Lecomte"), and [André Souris](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Souris "André Souris"). In 1927 they were joined by the writer [Louis Scutenaire](/wiki/Louis_Scutenaire "Louis Scutenaire"). They corresponded regularly with the Paris group, and in 1927 both Goemans and Magritte moved to Paris and frequented Breton's circle.[[19]](#cite_note-grove-19) The artists, with their roots in [Dada](/wiki/Dada "Dada") and [Cubism](/wiki/Cubism "Cubism"), the abstraction of [Wassily Kandinsky](/wiki/Wassily_Kandinsky "Wassily Kandinsky"), [Expressionism](/wiki/Expressionism "Expressionism"), and [Post-Impressionism](/wiki/Post-Impressionism "Post-Impressionism"), also reached to older "bloodlines" or [proto-surrealists](/wiki/Proto-Surrealism "Proto-Surrealism") such as [Hieronymus Bosch](/wiki/Hieronymus_Bosch "Hieronymus Bosch"), and the so-called primitive and naive arts.

[André Masson](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Masson "André Masson")'s [automatic drawings](/wiki/Surrealist_automatism#Automatic_drawing_and_painting "Surrealist automatism") of 1923 are often used as the point of the acceptance of visual arts and the break from Dada, since they reflect the influence of the idea of the [unconscious mind](/wiki/Unconscious_mind "Unconscious mind"). Another example is Giacometti's 1925 *Torso*, which marked his movement to simplified forms and inspiration from preclassical sculpture.

However, a striking example of the line used to divide Dada and Surrealism among art experts is the pairing of 1925's [*Little Machine Constructed by Minimax Dadamax in Person (Von minimax dadamax selbst konstruiertes maschinchen)*](/wiki/Little_Machine_Constructed_by_Minimax_Dadamax_in_Person "Little Machine Constructed by Minimax Dadamax in Person")[[34]](#cite_note-34) with *The Kiss (Le Baiser)*[[35]](#cite_note-35) from 1927 by Max Ernst.[*[clarify](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] The first is generally held to have a distance, and erotic subtext, whereas the second presents an erotic act openly and directly.[*[improper synthesis?](/wiki/Wikipedia:No_original_research#Synthesis_of_published_material "Wikipedia:No original research")*] In the second the influence of [Miró](/wiki/Joan_Mir%C3%B3 "Joan Miró") and the drawing style of [Picasso](/wiki/Pablo_Picasso "Pablo Picasso") is visible with the use of fluid curving and intersecting lines and colour, whereas the first takes a directness that would later be influential in movements such as [Pop art](/wiki/Pop_art "Pop art").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/b/b2/The_Red_Tower_by_Giorgio_de_Chirico.jpg/250px-The_Red_Tower_by_Giorgio_de_Chirico.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Red_Tower_by_Giorgio_de_Chirico.jpg)

[Giorgio de Chirico](/wiki/Giorgio_de_Chirico "Giorgio de Chirico"), *The Red Tower (La Tour Rouge)*, 1913, [Guggenheim Museum](/wiki/Solomon_R._Guggenheim_Museum "Solomon R. Guggenheim Museum")

Giorgio de Chirico, and his previous development of [metaphysical art](/wiki/Metaphysical_art "Metaphysical art"), was one of the important joining figures between the philosophical and visual aspects of Surrealism. Between 1911 and 1917, he adopted an unornamented depictional style whose surface would be adopted by others later. *The Red Tower (La tour rouge)* from 1913 shows the stark colour contrasts and illustrative style later adopted by Surrealist painters. His 1914 *The Nostalgia of the Poet (La Nostalgie du poète)*[[36]](#cite_note-36) has the figure turned away from the viewer, and the juxtaposition of a bust with glasses and a fish as a relief defies conventional explanation. He was also a writer whose novel *[Hebdomeros](/wiki/Hebdomeros "Hebdomeros")* presents a series of dreamscapes with an unusual use of punctuation, syntax, and grammar designed to create an atmosphere and frame its images. His images, including set designs for the [Ballets Russes](/wiki/Ballets_Russes "Ballets Russes"), would create a decorative form of Surrealism, and he would be an influence on the two artists who would be even more closely associated with Surrealism in the public mind: Dalí and Magritte. He would, however, leave the Surrealist group in 1928.

In 1924, Miró and Masson applied Surrealism to painting. The first Surrealist exhibition, *La Peinture Surrealiste*, was held at Galerie Pierre in Paris in 1925. It displayed works by Masson, [Man Ray](/wiki/Man_Ray "Man Ray"), [Paul Klee](/wiki/Paul_Klee "Paul Klee"), Miró, and others. The show confirmed that Surrealism had a component in the visual arts (though it had been initially debated whether this was possible), and techniques from Dada, such as [photomontage](/wiki/Photomontage "Photomontage"), were used. The following year, on March 26, 1926, Galerie Surréaliste opened with an exhibition by Man Ray. Breton published *Surrealism and Painting* in 1928 which summarized the movement to that point, though he continued to update the work until the 1960s.

### Surrealist literature

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Surrealism&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Surrealist literature")]

See also: [List of Surrealist poets](/wiki/List_of_Surrealist_poets "List of Surrealist poets")

The first Surrealist work, according to leader Breton, was *[Les Chants de Maldoror](/wiki/Les_Chants_de_Maldoror "Les Chants de Maldoror")*,[[37]](#cite_note-37) and the first work written and published by his group of *Surréalistes* was *[Les Champs Magnétiques](/wiki/Les_Champs_Magn%C3%A9tiques "Les Champs Magnétiques")* (May–June 1919).[[38]](#cite_note-38) *Littérature* contained automatist works and accounts of dreams. The magazine and the portfolio both showed their disdain for literal meanings given to objects and focused rather on the undertones; the poetic undercurrents present. Not only did they give emphasis to the poetic undercurrents, but also to the connotations and the overtones which "exist in ambiguous relationships to the visual images."[[39]](#cite_note-39)

Because Surrealist writers seldom, if ever, appear to organize their thoughts and the images they present, some people find much of their work difficult to parse. This notion however is a superficial comprehension, prompted no doubt by Breton's initial emphasis on automatic writing as the main route toward a higher reality. But—as in Breton's case—much of what is presented as purely automatic is actually edited and very "thought out". Breton himself later admitted that automatic writing's centrality had been overstated, and other elements were introduced, especially as the growing involvement of visual artists in the movement forced the issue, since automatic painting required a rather more strenuous set of approaches. Thus, such elements as collage were introduced, arising partly from an ideal of startling juxtapositions as revealed in [Pierre Reverdy](/wiki/Pierre_Reverdy "Pierre Reverdy")'s poetry. And—as in Magritte's case (where there is no obvious recourse to either automatic techniques or collage)—the very notion of convulsive joining became a tool for revelation in and of itself. Surrealism was meant to be always in flux—to be more modern than modern—and so it was natural there should be a rapid shuffling of the philosophy as new challenges arose. Artists such as Max Ernst and his surrealist collages demonstrate this shift to a more modern art form that also comments on society.[[40]](#cite_note-40)

Surrealists revived interest in Isidore Ducasse, known by his pseudonym [Comte de Lautréamont](/wiki/Comte_de_Lautr%C3%A9amont "Comte de Lautréamont"), and for the line "beautiful as the chance meeting on a dissecting table of a sewing machine and an umbrella", and [Arthur Rimbaud](/wiki/Arthur_Rimbaud "Arthur Rimbaud"), two late 19th-century writers believed to be the precursors of Surrealism.

Examples of Surrealist literature are Artaud's *Le Pèse-Nerfs* (1926), Aragon's *[Irene's Cunt](/wiki/Irene%27s_Cunt "Irene's Cunt")* (1927), Péret's *Death to the Pigs* (1929), Crevel's *Mr. Knife Miss Fork* (1931), [Sadegh Hedayat](/wiki/Sadegh_Hedayat "Sadegh Hedayat")'s *[The Blind Owl](/wiki/The_Blind_Owl "The Blind Owl")* (1937), and Breton's *Sur la route de San Romano* (1948).

*[La Révolution surréaliste](/wiki/La_R%C3%A9volution_surr%C3%A9aliste "La Révolution surréaliste")* continued publication into 1929 with most pages densely packed with columns of text, but which also included reproductions of art, among them works by de Chirico, Ernst, Masson, and Man Ray. Other works included books, poems, pamphlets, automatic texts and theoretical tracts.

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Surrealism&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Surrealist films")]

Main article: [Surrealist cinema](/wiki/Surrealist_cinema "Surrealist cinema")

Early films by Surrealists include:

* *[L'Étoile de mer](/wiki/L%27%C3%89toile_de_mer "L'Étoile de mer")* by [Man Ray](/wiki/Man_Ray "Man Ray") (1928)
