### Surrealist photography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Surrealism&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Surrealist photography")]

Famous Surrealist photographers are the French [Dora Maar](/wiki/Dora_Maar "Dora Maar"), the American [Man Ray](/wiki/Man_Ray "Man Ray"), the French/Hungarian [Brassaï](/wiki/Brassa%C3%AF "Brassaï"), French [Claude Cahun](/wiki/Claude_Cahun "Claude Cahun") and the Dutch [Emiel van Moerkerken](/wiki/Emiel_van_Moerkerken "Emiel van Moerkerken").[[41]](#cite_note-Moerkerken-41)[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[43]](#cite_note-43) In Japan (see [Surrealism in Japan](/wiki/Surrealism_in_Japan "Surrealism in Japan")), the 1937 exhibition *[Kaigai Chōgenjitsushugi Sakuhin Ten](/wiki/Kaigai_Ch%C5%8Dgenjitsushugi_Sakuhinten "Kaigai Chōgenjitsushugi Sakuhinten")* (*Exhibition of Overseas Surrealist Works*)—described as Japan’s first Surrealism “international” exhibition—consisted largely of photographs, many of them reproductions of Surrealist paintings and objects.[[44]](#cite_note-Ishii2020KaigaiSurrealism2-44) Surrealist photography also influenced avant-garde circles there; for example, the Japanese poet and photographer [Kansuke Yamamoto](/wiki/Kansuke_Yamamoto_(artist) "Kansuke Yamamoto (artist)") participated in the [Nagoya Photo Avant-Garde](/wiki/Nagoya_Photo_Avant-Garde "Nagoya Photo Avant-Garde").[[45]](#cite_note-TOPMuseum_AvantGardeRising_Nagoya-45)[[46]](#cite_note-Getty_ModernDivide_Yamamoto-46)

### Surrealist theatre

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Surrealism&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Surrealist theatre")]

Further information: [Theatre of Cruelty](/wiki/Theatre_of_Cruelty "Theatre of Cruelty")

The word *surrealist* was first used by Apollinaire to describe his 1917 play *[Les Mamelles de Tirésias](/wiki/The_Breasts_of_Tiresias "The Breasts of Tiresias")* ("The Breasts of Tiresias"), which was later [adapted into an opera](/wiki/Les_mamelles_de_Tir%C3%A9sias "Les mamelles de Tirésias") by [Francis Poulenc](/wiki/Francis_Poulenc "Francis Poulenc").[[47]](#cite_note-47)

[Roger Vitrac](/wiki/Roger_Vitrac "Roger Vitrac")'s *The Mysteries of Love* (1927) and *Victor, or The Children Take Over* (1928) are often considered the best examples of Surrealist theatre, despite his expulsion from the movement in 1926.[[48]](#cite_note-48)[[49]](#cite_note-49)[[50]](#cite_note-50) The plays were staged at the [Theatre Alfred Jarry](/wiki/Theatre_Alfred_Jarry "Theatre Alfred Jarry"), the theatre Vitrac co-founded with [Antonin Artaud](/wiki/Antonin_Artaud "Antonin Artaud"), another early Surrealist who was expelled from the movement.[[51]](#cite_note-51)

Following his collaboration with Vitrac, Artaud would extend Surrealist thought through his theory of the [Theatre of Cruelty](/wiki/Theatre_of_Cruelty "Theatre of Cruelty"). Artaud rejected the majority of Western theatre as a perversion of its original intent, which he felt should be a mystical, metaphysical experience.[[52]](#cite_note-52) Instead, he envisioned a theatre that would be immediate and direct, linking the unconscious minds of performers and spectators in a sort of ritual event, Artaud created in which emotions, feelings, and the metaphysical were expressed not through language but physically, creating a mythological, archetypal, allegorical vision, closely related to the world of dreams.[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54)

The Spanish playwright and director [Federico García Lorca](/wiki/Federico_Garc%C3%ADa_Lorca "Federico García Lorca"), also experimented with surrealism, particularly in his plays *[The Public](/wiki/The_Public_(play) "The Public (play)")* (1930), *[When Five Years Pass](/wiki/When_Five_Years_Pass "When Five Years Pass")* (1931), and *[Play Without a Title](/wiki/Play_Without_a_Title "Play Without a Title")* (1935). Other surrealist plays include Aragon's *Backs to the Wall* (1925).[[55]](#cite_note-55) [Gertrude Stein](/wiki/Gertrude_Stein "Gertrude Stein")'s opera *[Doctor Faustus Lights the Lights](/wiki/Doctor_Faustus_Lights_the_Lights "Doctor Faustus Lights the Lights")* (1938) has also been described as "American Surrealism", though it is also related to a theatrical form of [cubism](/wiki/Cubism "Cubism").[[56]](#cite_note-56)

### Surrealist music

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Surrealism&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Surrealist music")]

Main article: [Surrealist music](/wiki/Surrealist_music "Surrealist music")

In the 1920s several composers were influenced by Surrealism, or by individuals in the Surrealist movement. Among them were [Bohuslav Martinů](/wiki/Bohuslav_Martin%C5%AF "Bohuslav Martinů"), [André Souris](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Souris "André Souris"), [Erik Satie](/wiki/Erik_Satie "Erik Satie"),[[57]](#cite_note-57) [Francis Poulenc](/wiki/Francis_Poulenc "Francis Poulenc"),[[58]](#cite_note-58)[[59]](#cite_note-59) and [Edgard Varèse](/wiki/Edgard_Var%C3%A8se "Edgard Varèse"), who stated that his work *Arcana* was drawn from a dream sequence.[[60]](#cite_note-60) Souris in particular was associated with the movement: he had a long relationship with Magritte, and worked on [Paul Nougé](/wiki/Paul_Noug%C3%A9 "Paul Nougé")'s publication *Adieu Marie*. Music by composers from across the twentieth century have been associated with surrealist principles, including [Pierre Boulez](/wiki/Pierre_Boulez "Pierre Boulez"),[[61]](#cite_note-61) [György Ligeti](/wiki/Gy%C3%B6rgy_Ligeti "György Ligeti"),[[62]](#cite_note-62) [Mauricio Kagel](/wiki/Mauricio_Kagel "Mauricio Kagel"), [Olivier Messiaen](/wiki/Olivier_Messiaen "Olivier Messiaen"),[[63]](#cite_note-63) and [Thomas Adès](/wiki/Thomas_Ad%C3%A8s "Thomas Adès").[[64]](#cite_note-64)[[65]](#cite_note-65)

[Germaine Tailleferre](/wiki/Germaine_Tailleferre "Germaine Tailleferre") of the French group [Les Six](/wiki/Les_Six "Les Six") wrote several works which could be considered to be inspired by Surrealism,[[66]](#cite_note-66) including the 1948 ballet *Paris-Magie* (scenario by [Lise Deharme](/wiki/Lise_Deharme "Lise Deharme")), the operas *La Petite Sirène* (book by Philippe Soupault) and *Le Maître* (book by Eugène Ionesco).[[67]](#cite_note-67) Tailleferre also wrote popular songs to texts by Claude Marci, the wife of Henri Jeanson, whose portrait had been painted by Magritte in the 1930s.

Even though Breton by 1946 responded rather negatively to the subject of music with his essay *Silence is Golden*, later Surrealists, such as [Paul Garon](/wiki/Paul_Garon "Paul Garon"), have been interested in—and found parallels to—Surrealism in the improvisation of [jazz](/wiki/Jazz "Jazz") and the [blues](/wiki/Blues "Blues"). Jazz and blues musicians have occasionally reciprocated this interest. For example, the [1976 World Surrealist Exhibition](/wiki/1976_World_Surrealist_Exhibition "1976 World Surrealist Exhibition") included performances by [David "Honeyboy" Edwards](/wiki/David_%22Honeyboy%22_Edwards "David \"Honeyboy\" Edwards").

## Surrealism and international politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Surrealism&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Surrealism and international politics")]

Surrealism as a political force developed unevenly around the world: in some places more emphasis was on artistic practices, in other places on political practices, and in other places still, Surrealist praxis looked to supersede both the arts and politics. During the 1930s, the Surrealist idea spread from Europe to North America, South America (founding of the *[Mandrágora](/wiki/Mandr%C3%A1gora "Mandrágora")* group in Chile in 1938), [Central America](/wiki/Central_America "Central America"), [the Caribbean](/wiki/The_Caribbean "The Caribbean"), and throughout Asia, as both an artistic idea and as an ideology of political change.[[68]](#cite_note-Bauduin_et_al-68)[[69]](#cite_note-Spiteri-69)

Politically, Surrealism was [Trotskyist](/wiki/Trotskyism "Trotskyism"), [communist](/wiki/Communist "Communist"), or [anarchist](/wiki/Anarchist "Anarchist").[[68]](#cite_note-Bauduin_et_al-68) The split from Dada has been characterised as a split between anarchists and communists, with the Surrealists as communist. Breton and his comrades supported [Leon Trotsky](/wiki/Leon_Trotsky "Leon Trotsky") and his [International Left Opposition](/wiki/International_Left_Opposition "International Left Opposition") for a while, though there was an openness to anarchism that manifested more fully after World War II. Some Surrealists, such as [Benjamin Péret](/wiki/Benjamin_P%C3%A9ret "Benjamin Péret"), Mary Low, and Juan Breá, aligned with forms of [left communism](/wiki/Left_communism "Left communism"). When the Dutch surrealist photographer [Emiel van Moerkerken](https://nl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emiel_van_Moerkerken "nl:Emiel van Moerkerken") came to Breton, he did not want to sign the manifesto because he was not a Trotskyist. For Breton being a communist was not enough. Breton denied Van Moerkerken's pictures for a publication afterwards.[[41]](#cite_note-Moerkerken-41) This caused a split in surrealism. Others fought for complete liberty from political ideologies, like [Wolfgang Paalen](/wiki/Wolfgang_Paalen "Wolfgang Paalen"), who, after Trotsky's assassination in Mexico, prepared a schism between art and politics through his counter-surrealist art-magazine *[DYN](/wiki/DYN_(magazine) "DYN (magazine)")* and so prepared the ground for the abstract expressionists. Dalí supported capitalism and the fascist dictatorship of [Francisco Franco](/wiki/Francisco_Franco "Francisco Franco") but cannot be said to represent a trend in Surrealism in this respect; in fact, he was considered, by Breton and his associates, to have betrayed and left Surrealism. Benjamin Péret, Mary Low, Juan Breá, and Spanish-native [Eugenio Fernández Granell](/wiki/Eugenio_Granell "Eugenio Granell") joined the [POUM](/wiki/Workers%27_Party_of_Marxist_Unification "Workers' Party of Marxist Unification") during the [Spanish Civil War](/wiki/Spanish_Civil_War "Spanish Civil War").[[68]](#cite_note-Bauduin_et_al-68)[[69]](#cite_note-Spiteri-69)

Breton's followers, along with the [Communist Party](/wiki/French_Communist_Party "French Communist Party"), were working for the "liberation of man". However, Breton's group refused to prioritize the [proletarian](/wiki/Proletarian "Proletarian") struggle over radical creation such that their struggles with the Party made the late 1920s a turbulent time for both. Many individuals closely associated with Breton, notably Aragon, left his group to work more closely with the Communists.[[68]](#cite_note-Bauduin_et_al-68)[[69]](#cite_note-Spiteri-69)

Surrealists have often sought to link their efforts with political ideals and activities. In the *Declaration of January 27, 1925*,[[70]](#cite_note-70) for example, members of the Paris-based [Bureau of Surrealist Research](/wiki/Bureau_of_Surrealist_Research "Bureau of Surrealist Research") (including Breton, Aragon and Artaud, as well as some two dozen others) declared their affinity for revolutionary politics. While this was initially a somewhat vague formulation, by the 1930s many Surrealists had strongly identified themselves with communism. The foremost document of this tendency within Surrealism is the *Manifesto for a Free Revolutionary Art*,[[71]](#cite_note-71) published under the names of Breton and [Diego Rivera](/wiki/Diego_Rivera "Diego Rivera"), but actually co-authored by Breton and [Leon Trotsky](/wiki/Leon_Trotsky "Leon Trotsky").[[72]](#cite_note-72)

However, in 1933 the Surrealists' assertion that a "[proletarian literature](/wiki/Proletarian_literature "Proletarian literature")" within a capitalist society was impossible led to their break with the Association des Ecrivains et Artistes Révolutionnaires, and the expulsion of Breton, Éluard and Crevel from the Communist Party.[[19]](#cite_note-grove-19)

In 1925, the Paris Surrealist group and the extreme left of the [French Communist Party](/wiki/French_Communist_Party "French Communist Party") came together to support [Abd-el-Krim](/wiki/Muhammad_Ibn_%27Abd_al-Karim_al-Khattabi "Muhammad Ibn 'Abd al-Karim al-Khattabi"), leader of the [Rif](/wiki/Rif "Rif") uprising against French colonialism in [Morocco](/wiki/Morocco "Morocco"). In an open letter to writer and French ambassador to Japan, [Paul Claudel](/wiki/Paul_Claudel "Paul Claudel"), the Paris group announced:

> We Surrealists pronounced ourselves in favour of changing the imperialist war, in its chronic and colonial form, into a civil war. Thus we placed our energies at the disposal of the revolution, of the proletariat and its struggles, and defined our attitude towards the colonial problem, and hence towards the colour question.

The anticolonial revolutionary and proletarian politics of "Murderous Humanitarianism" (1932) which was drafted mainly by Crevel, signed by Breton, Éluard, Péret, Tanguy, and the Martiniquan Surrealists [Pierre Yoyotte](/w/index.php?title=Pierre_Yoyotte&action=edit&redlink=1 "Pierre Yoyotte (page does not exist)") and [J.M. Monnerot](/w/index.php?title=J.M._Monnerot&action=edit&redlink=1 "J.M. Monnerot (page does not exist)") perhaps makes it the original document of what is later called "black Surrealism",[[73]](#cite_note-73) although it is the contact between [Aimé Césaire](/wiki/Aim%C3%A9_C%C3%A9saire "Aimé Césaire") and Breton in the 1940s in [Martinique](/wiki/Martinique "Martinique") that really lead to the communication of what is known as "black Surrealism".

Anticolonial revolutionary writers in the [Négritude](/wiki/N%C3%A9gritude "Négritude") movement of [Martinique](/wiki/Martinique "Martinique"), a French colony at the time, took up Surrealism as a revolutionary method – a critique of European culture and a radical subjective. This linked with other Surrealists and was very important for the subsequent development of Surrealism as a revolutionary praxis. The journal *[Tropiques](/wiki/Tropiques "Tropiques")*, featuring the work of Césaire along with [Suzanne Césaire](/wiki/Suzanne_C%C3%A9saire "Suzanne Césaire"), [René Ménil](/wiki/Ren%C3%A9_M%C3%A9nil "René Ménil"), [Lucie Thésée](/wiki/Lucie_Th%C3%A9s%C3%A9e "Lucie Thésée"), [Aristide Maugée](/w/index.php?title=Aristide_Maug%C3%A9e&action=edit&redlink=1 "Aristide Maugée (page does not exist)") and others, was first published in 1941.[[74]](#cite_note-74)

In 1938 André Breton traveled with his wife, the painter [Jacqueline Lamba](/wiki/Jacqueline_Lamba "Jacqueline Lamba"), to [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico") to meet Trotsky (staying as the guest of Diego Rivera's former wife Guadalupe Marin), and there he met [Frida Kahlo](/wiki/Frida_Kahlo "Frida Kahlo") and saw her paintings for the first time. Breton declared Kahlo to be an "innate" Surrealist painter.[[75]](#cite_note-75)

### Internal politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Surrealism&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Internal politics")]

In 1929 the satellite group associated with the journal *Le Grand Jeu*, including [Roger Gilbert-Lecomte](/wiki/Roger_Gilbert-Lecomte "Roger Gilbert-Lecomte"), [Maurice Henry](/wiki/Maurice_Henry "Maurice Henry") and the Czech painter [Josef Sima](/wiki/Josef_Sima "Josef Sima"), was ostracized. Also in February, Breton asked Surrealists to assess their "degree of moral competence", and theoretical refinements included in the second *[manifeste du surréalisme](/wiki/Surrealist_Manifesto "Surrealist Manifesto")* excluded anyone reluctant to commit to collective action, a list which included Leiris, Limbour, Morise, Baron, Queneau, Prévert, Desnos, Masson and Boiffard. Excluded members launched a counterattack, sharply criticizing Breton in the pamphlet *[Un Cadavre](/wiki/Un_Cadavre "Un Cadavre")*, which featured a picture of Breton wearing a [crown of thorns](/wiki/Crown_of_thorns "Crown of thorns"). The pamphlet drew upon an earlier act of subversion by likening Breton to [Anatole France](/wiki/Anatole_France "Anatole France"), whose unquestioned value Breton had challenged in 1924.

The disunion of 1929–30 and the effects of *Un Cadavre* had very little negative impact upon Surrealism as Breton saw it, since core figures such as Aragon, Crevel, Dalí and Buñuel remained true to the idea of group action, at least for the time being. The success (or the controversy) of Dalí and Buñuel's film *[L'Age d'Or](/wiki/L%27Age_d%27Or "L'Age d'Or")* in December 1930 had a regenerative effect, drawing a number of new recruits, and encouraging countless new artistic works the following year and throughout the 1930s.

Disgruntled surrealists moved to the periodical *[Documents](/wiki/Documents_(magazine) "Documents (magazine)")*, edited by [Georges Bataille](/wiki/Georges_Bataille "Georges Bataille"), whose anti-idealist materialism formed a hybrid Surrealism intending to expose the base instincts of humans.[[19]](#cite_note-grove-19)[[76]](#cite_note-pompidou-76) To the dismay of many, *Documents* fizzled out in 1931, just as Surrealism seemed to be gathering more steam.

There were a number of reconciliations after this period of disunion, such as between Breton and Bataille, while Aragon left the group after committing himself to the [French Communist Party](/wiki/French_Communist_Party "French Communist Party") in 1932. More members were ousted over the years for a variety of infractions, both political and personal, while others left in pursuit of their own style.

By the end of World War II, the surrealist group led by André Breton decided to explicitly embrace anarchism. In 1952 Breton wrote "It was in the black mirror of anarchism that surrealism first recognised itself."[[77]](#cite_note-anarchosurrealism-77) Breton was consistent in his support for the [francophone Anarchist Federation](/wiki/F%C3%A9d%C3%A9ration_Anarchiste "Fédération Anarchiste") and he continued to offer his solidarity after the [Platformists](/wiki/Platformism "Platformism") supporting Fontenis transformed the FA into the Fédération Communiste Libertaire. He was one of the few intellectuals who continued to offer his support to the FCL during the Algerian war when the FCL suffered severe repression and was forced underground. He sheltered Fontenis whilst he was in hiding. He refused to take sides on the splits in the French anarchist movement and both he and Peret expressed solidarity as well with the new [Fédération anarchiste](/wiki/F%C3%A9d%C3%A9ration_anarchiste "Fédération anarchiste") set up by the synthesist anarchists and worked in the Antifascist Committees of the 60s alongside the FA.[[77]](#cite_note-anarchosurrealism-77)

## Golden age

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Surrealism&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Golden age")]

Throughout the 1930s, Surrealism continued to become more visible to the public at large. A [Surrealist group developed in London](/wiki/British_Surrealist_Group "British Surrealist Group") and, according to Breton, their 1936 [London International Surrealist Exhibition](/wiki/London_International_Surrealist_Exhibition "London International Surrealist Exhibition") was a high-water mark of the period and became the model for international exhibitions. Another English Surrealist group developed [in Birmingham](/wiki/Birmingham_Surrealists "Birmingham Surrealists"), meanwhile, and was distinguished by its opposition to the London surrealists and preferences for surrealism's French heartland. The two groups would reconcile later in the decade.

Dalí and Magritte created the most widely recognized images of the movement. Dalí joined the group in 1929 and participated in the rapid establishment of the visual style between 1930 and 1935.

Surrealism as a visual movement had found a method: to expose psychological truth; stripping ordinary objects of their normal significance, to create a compelling image that was beyond ordinary formal organization, in order to evoke empathy from the viewer.

1931 was a year when several Surrealist painters produced works which marked turning points in their stylistic evolution: Magritte's *Voice of Space (La Voix des airs)*[[78]](#cite_note-78) is an example of this process, where three large spheres representing bells hang above a landscape. Another Surrealist landscape from this same year is Yves Tanguy's *[Promontory Palace (Palais promontoire)](/w/index.php?title=File:Premontory_Palace.jpg&action=edit&redlink=1 "File:Premontory Palace.jpg (page does not exist)")*, with its molten forms and liquid shapes. Liquid shapes became the trademark of Dalí, particularly in his *[The Persistence of Memory](/wiki/The_Persistence_of_Memory "The Persistence of Memory")*, which features the image of watches that sag as if they were melting.

The characteristics of this style—a combination of the depictive, the abstract, and the psychological—came to stand for the alienation which many people felt in the [modern](/wiki/Modernism "Modernism") period, combined with the sense of reaching more deeply into the psyche, to be "made whole with one's individuality".

Between 1930 and 1933, the Surrealist Group in Paris issued the periodical *[Le Surréalisme au service de la révolution](/wiki/Le_Surr%C3%A9alisme_au_service_de_la_r%C3%A9volution "Le Surréalisme au service de la révolution")* as the successor of *La Révolution surréaliste*.

From 1936 through 1938 [Wolfgang Paalen](/wiki/Wolfgang_Paalen "Wolfgang Paalen"), [Gordon Onslow Ford](/wiki/Gordon_Onslow_Ford "Gordon Onslow Ford"), and [Roberto Matta](/wiki/Roberto_Matta "Roberto Matta") joined the group. Paalen contributed [Fumage](/wiki/Fumage "Fumage") and Onslow Ford [Coulage](/wiki/Coulage "Coulage") as new pictorial automatic techniques.

Long after personal, political and professional tensions fragmented the Surrealist group, Magritte and Dalí continued to define a visual program in the arts. This program reached beyond painting, to encompass photography as well, as can be seen from a Man Ray self-portrait, whose use of assemblage influenced [Robert Rauschenberg](/wiki/Robert_Rauschenberg "Robert Rauschenberg")'s collage boxes.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/d/db/L%27Ange_du_Foyeur.jpg/250px-L%27Ange_du_Foyeur.jpg)](/wiki/File:L%27Ange_du_Foyeur.jpg)

[Max Ernst](/wiki/Max_Ernst "Max Ernst"), *L'Ange du Foyer ou le Triomphe du Surréalisme* (1937), private collection

During the 1930s [Peggy Guggenheim](/wiki/Peggy_Guggenheim "Peggy Guggenheim"), an important American art collector, married Max Ernst and began promoting work by other Surrealists such as Yves Tanguy and the British artist [John Tunnard](/wiki/John_Tunnard "John Tunnard").

**Major exhibitions in the 1930s**

* 1936 – *[London International Surrealist Exhibition](/wiki/London_International_Surrealist_Exhibition "London International Surrealist Exhibition")* is organised in London by the art historian [Herbert Read](/wiki/Herbert_Read "Herbert Read"), with an introduction by André Breton.
* 1936 – [Museum of Modern Art](/wiki/Museum_of_Modern_Art "Museum of Modern Art") in New York shows the exhibition *[Fantastic Art](/w/index.php?title=Fantastic_Art&action=edit&redlink=1 "Fantastic Art (page does not exist)"), Dada and Surrealism*.
* 1938 – A new *[Exposition Internationale du Surréalisme](/wiki/Exposition_Internationale_du_Surr%C3%A9alisme "Exposition Internationale du Surréalisme")* was held at the Beaux-arts Gallery, Paris, with more than 60 artists from different countries, and showed around 300 paintings, objects, collages, photographs and installations. The Surrealists wanted to create an exhibition which in itself would be a creative act and called on Marcel Duchamp, Wolfgang Paalen, Man Ray and others to do so. At the exhibition's entrance Salvador Dalí placed his [Rainy Taxi](/wiki/Rainy_Taxi "Rainy Taxi") (an old taxi rigged to produce a steady drizzle of water down the inside of the windows, and a shark-headed creature in the driver's seat and a blond mannequin crawling with live snails in the back) greeted the patrons who were in full evening dress. *Surrealist Street* filled one side of the lobby with mannequins dressed by various Surrealists. Paalen and Duchamp designed the main hall to seem like cave with 1,200 coal bags suspended from the ceiling over a coal brazier with a single light bulb which provided the only lighting, as well as the floor covered with humid leaves and mud.[[79]](#cite_note-79) The patrons were given flashlights with which to view the art. On the floor Wolfgang Paalen created a small lake with grasses and the aroma of roasting coffee filled the air. Much to the Surrealists' satisfaction the exhibition scandalized the viewers.[[33]](#cite_note-tomkins-33)

### World War II and the Post War period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Surrealism&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: World War II and the Post War period")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/a/ae/Indefinite_Divisibility.jpg/250px-Indefinite_Divisibility.jpg)](/wiki/File:Indefinite_Divisibility.jpg)

[Yves Tanguy](/wiki/Yves_Tanguy "Yves Tanguy") *Indefinite Divisibility*, 1942, [Albright Knox Art Gallery](/wiki/Albright_Knox_Art_Gallery "Albright Knox Art Gallery"), Buffalo, New York

World War II created havoc not only for the general population of Europe but especially for the European artists and writers that opposed Fascism and Nazism. Many important artists fled to North America and relative safety in the United States. The art community in [New York City](/wiki/Culture_of_New_York_City "Culture of New York City") in particular was already grappling with Surrealist ideas and several artists like [Arshile Gorky](/wiki/Arshile_Gorky "Arshile Gorky"), [Jackson Pollock](/wiki/Jackson_Pollock "Jackson Pollock"), and [Robert Motherwell](/wiki/Robert_Motherwell "Robert Motherwell") converged closely with the surrealist artists themselves, albeit with some suspicion and reservations. Ideas concerning the unconscious and dream imagery were quickly embraced. By the Second World War, the taste of the American [avant-garde](/wiki/Avant-garde "Avant-garde") in New York swung decisively towards [Abstract Expressionism](/wiki/Abstract_Expressionism "Abstract Expressionism") with the support of key taste makers, including [Peggy Guggenheim](/wiki/Peggy_Guggenheim "Peggy Guggenheim"), [Leo Steinberg](/wiki/Leo_Steinberg "Leo Steinberg") and [Clement Greenberg](/wiki/Clement_Greenberg "Clement Greenberg"). However, it should not be easily forgotten that Abstract Expressionism itself grew directly out of the meeting of American (particularly New York) artists with European Surrealists self-exiled during World War II. In particular, Gorky and Paalen influenced the development of this American art form, which, as Surrealism did, celebrated the instantaneous human act as the well-spring of creativity. The early work of many Abstract Expressionists reveals a tight bond between the more superficial aspects of both movements, and the emergence (at a later date) of aspects of [Dadaistic](/wiki/Dadaist "Dadaist") humor in such artists as [Rauschenberg](/wiki/Rauschenberg "Rauschenberg") sheds an even starker light upon the connection. Up until the emergence of [Pop Art](/wiki/Pop_Art "Pop Art"), Surrealism can be seen to have been the single most important influence on the sudden growth in American arts, and even in Pop, some of the humor manifested in Surrealism can be found, often turned to a cultural criticism.

The Second World War overshadowed, for a time, almost all intellectual and artistic production. In 1939 Wolfgang Paalen was the first to leave Paris for the New World as exile. After a long trip through the forests of British Columbia, he settled in Mexico and founded his influential art-magazine [Dyn](/wiki/DYN_(journal) "DYN (journal)"). In 1940 Yves Tanguy married American Surrealist painter [Kay Sage](/wiki/Kay_Sage "Kay Sage"). In 1941, Breton went to the United States, where he co-founded the short-lived magazine *[VVV](/wiki/VVV_(magazine) "VVV (magazine)")* with Max Ernst, Marcel Duchamp, and the American artist [David Hare](/wiki/David_Hare_(artist) "David Hare (artist)"). However, it was the American poet, [Charles Henri Ford](/wiki/Charles_Henri_Ford "Charles Henri Ford"), and his magazine *[View](/wiki/View_(magazine) "View (magazine)")* which offered Breton a channel for promoting Surrealism in the United States. The *View* special issue on Duchamp was crucial for the public understanding of Surrealism in America. It stressed his connections to Surrealist methods, offered interpretations of his work by Breton, as well as Breton's view that Duchamp represented the bridge between early modern movements, such as [Futurism](/wiki/Futurism_(art) "Futurism (art)") and [Cubism](/wiki/Cubism "Cubism"), to Surrealism. Wolfgang Paalen left the group in 1942 due to political/philosophical differences with Breton.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/7/7e/The_Conspirators_by_Colin_Middleton_1942lr.jpg/330px-The_Conspirators_by_Colin_Middleton_1942lr.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Conspirators_by_Colin_Middleton_1942lr.jpg)

*The Conspirators* by Colin Middleton (1942), the Irish Surrealist's response to the [Belfast Blitz](/wiki/Belfast_Blitz "Belfast Blitz")

Though the war proved disruptive for Surrealism, the works continued. Many Surrealist artists continued to explore their vocabularies, including Magritte. Many members of the Surrealist movement continued to correspond and meet. While Dalí may have been excommunicated by Breton, he neither abandoned his themes from the 1930s, including references to the "persistence of time" in a later painting, nor did he become a depictive pompier. His classic period did not represent so sharp a break with the past as some descriptions of his work might portray, and some, such as [André Thirion](/wiki/Andr%C3%A9_Thirion "André Thirion"), argued that there were works of his after this period that continued to have some relevance for the movement. When the war reached Ireland with the [Belfast Blitz](/wiki/Belfast_Blitz "Belfast Blitz") in May 1941, [Colin Middleton](/wiki/Colin_Middleton "Colin Middleton"), who had experimented with surrealist themes in the 1930s, responded with a series of dark works reflecting the shocked state of the people of the city. These were exhibited at the [Belfast Municipal Gallery and Museum](/wiki/Ulster_Museum "Ulster Museum") after its restoration in 1943, following near destruction in the blitz.[[80]](#cite_note-PatMurphy-80)

During the 1940s Surrealism's influence was also felt in England, America and the Netherlands where Gertrude Pape and her husband Theo van Baaren helped to popularize it in their publication The Clean Handkerchief.[[81]](#cite_note-81) [Mark Rothko](/wiki/Mark_Rothko "Mark Rothko") took an interest in [biomorphic](/wiki/Biomorphism "Biomorphism") figures, and in England [Henry Moore](/wiki/Henry_Moore "Henry Moore"), [Lucian Freud](/wiki/Lucian_Freud "Lucian Freud"), [Francis Bacon](/wiki/Francis_Bacon_(artist) "Francis Bacon (artist)") and [Paul Nash](/wiki/Paul_Nash_(artist) "Paul Nash (artist)") used or experimented with Surrealist techniques. However, [Conroy Maddox](/wiki/Conroy_Maddox "Conroy Maddox"), one of the first British Surrealists whose work in this genre dated from 1935, remained within the movement, and organized an exhibition of current Surrealist work in 1978 in response to an earlier show which infuriated him because it did not properly represent Surrealism. Maddox's exhibition, titled *Surrealism Unlimited*, was held in Paris and attracted international attention. He held his last one-man show in 2002, and died three years later. Magritte's work became more realistic in its depiction of actual objects, while maintaining the element of juxtaposition, such as in 1951's *Personal Values (Les Valeurs Personnelles)*[[82]](#cite_note-82) and 1954's *Empire of Light (L’Empire des lumières)*.[[83]](#cite_note-83) Magritte continued to produce works which have entered artistic vocabulary, such as *Castle in the Pyrenees (Le Château des Pyrénées)*,[[84]](#cite_note-84) which refers back to *Voix* from 1931, in its suspension over a landscape.

Other figures from the Surrealist movement were expelled. Several of these artists, like [Roberto Matta](/wiki/Roberto_Matta "Roberto Matta") (by his own description) "remained close to Surrealism".[[33]](#cite_note-tomkins-33) Frida Kahlo should be mentioned. She had a New York solo exhibition in 1938 with 25 paintings, encouraged by Breton himself.

In [South America](/wiki/South_America "South America"), the Chilean Surrealist group [La Mandrágora](/wiki/La_Mandr%C3%A1gora "La Mandrágora") was founded in 1938 by [Braulio Arenas](/wiki/Braulio_Arenas "Braulio Arenas"), [Teófilo Cid](/wiki/Te%C3%B3filo_Cid "Teófilo Cid"), and [Enrique Gómez Correa](/wiki/Enrique_G%C3%B3mez_Correa "Enrique Gómez Correa"). The group published an eponymous review and was instrumental in introducing Surrealist thought to the [Southern Cone](/wiki/Southern_Cone "Southern Cone"), often supported by the poet [Vicente Huidobro](/wiki/Vicente_Huidobro "Vicente Huidobro").[[85]](#cite_note-85) Along with Matta, the Mandragora would be a significant literary influence on [Roberto Bolaño](/wiki/Roberto_Bola%C3%B1o "Roberto Bolaño").

After the crushing of the [Hungarian Revolution of 1956](/wiki/Hungarian_Revolution_of_1956 "Hungarian Revolution of 1956"), [Endre Rozsda](/wiki/Endre_Rozsda "Endre Rozsda") returned to Paris to continue creating his own word that had been transcended the surrealism. The preface to his first exhibition in the Furstenberg Gallery (1957) was written by Breton yet.[[86]](#cite_note-breton-86)

Many new artists explicitly took up the Surrealist banner. [Dorothea Tanning](/wiki/Dorothea_Tanning "Dorothea Tanning") and [Louise Bourgeois](/wiki/Louise_Bourgeois "Louise Bourgeois") continued to work, for example, with Tanning's *Rainy Day Canape* from 1970. Duchamp continued to produce sculpture in secret, including his installation depicting a woman realistically, viewed through a peephole: [Étant donnés](/wiki/%C3%89tant_donn%C3%A9s "Étant donnés").

Breton continued to write and espouse the importance of liberating the human mind, as with the publication *[The Tower of Light](/w/index.php?title=The_Tower_of_Light&action=edit&redlink=1 "The Tower of Light (page does not exist)")* in 1952. Breton's return to France after the War, began a new phase of Surrealist activity in Paris, and his critiques of rationalism and dualism found a new audience. Breton insisted that Surrealism was an ongoing revolt against the reduction of humanity to market relationships, religious gestures and misery and to espouse the importance of liberating the human mind.

**Major exhibitions of the 1940s, '50s and '60s**

* 1942 – *First Papers of Surrealism* – New York – The Surrealists again called on Duchamp to design an exhibition. This time he wove a 3-dimensional web of string throughout the rooms of the space, in some cases making it almost impossible to see the works.[[87]](#cite_note-87) He made a secret arrangement with an associate's son to bring his friends to the opening of the show, so that when the finely dressed patrons arrived, they found a dozen children in athletic clothes kicking and passing balls and skipping rope. His design for the show's catalog included "found", rather than posed, photographs of the artists.[[33]](#cite_note-tomkins-33)
* 1947 – International Surrealist Exhibition – Galerie Maeght, Paris[[88]](#cite_note-88)
* 1959 – International Surrealist Exhibition – Paris
* 1960 – *Surrealist Intrusion in the Enchanters' Domain* – New York

## Post-Breton Surrealism

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Surrealism&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Post-Breton Surrealism")]

The [Afro-Surrealism](/wiki/Afro-Surrealism "Afro-Surrealism") is an art and literary movement primarily in the [African diaspora](/wiki/African_diaspora "African diaspora"), inspired by [Négritude](/wiki/N%C3%A9gritude "Négritude") and [postcolonial literature](/wiki/Postcolonial_literature "Postcolonial literature") and partially coinciding with them.[[89]](#cite_note-Spenser-89)

In the 1960s, the artists and writers associated with the [Situationist International](/wiki/Situationist_International "Situationist International") were closely associated with Surrealism. While [Guy Debord](/wiki/Guy_Debord "Guy Debord") was critical of and distanced himself from Surrealism, others, such as [Asger Jorn](/wiki/Asger_Jorn "Asger Jorn"), were explicitly using Surrealist techniques and methods. [The events of May 1968](/wiki/May_1968_events_in_France "May 1968 events in France") in France included a number of Surrealist ideas, and among the slogans the students spray-painted on the walls of the Sorbonne were familiar Surrealist ones. [Joan Miró](/wiki/Joan_Mir%C3%B3 "Joan Miró") would commemorate this in a painting titled *May 1968.* There were also groups who associated with both currents and were more attached to Surrealism, such as the [Revolutionary Surrealist Group](/w/index.php?title=Revolutionary_Surrealist_Group&action=edit&redlink=1 "Revolutionary Surrealist Group (page does not exist)").

During the 1980s, behind the [Iron Curtain](/wiki/Iron_Curtain "Iron Curtain"), Surrealism again entered into politics with an underground artistic opposition movement known as the [Orange Alternative](/wiki/Orange_Alternative "Orange Alternative"). The Orange Alternative was created in 1981 by [Waldemar Fydrych](/wiki/Waldemar_Fydrych "Waldemar Fydrych") (alias 'Major'), a graduate of history and art history at the University of [Wrocław](/wiki/Wroc%C5%82aw "Wrocław"). They used Surrealist symbolism and terminology in their large-scale happenings organized in the major Polish cities during the [Jaruzelski](/wiki/Jaruzelski "Jaruzelski") regime and painted Surrealist graffiti on spots covering up anti-regime slogans. Major himself was the author of a "Manifesto of Socialist Surrealism". In this manifesto, he stated that the socialist (communist) system had become so Surrealistic that it could be seen as an expression of art itself.

Surrealistic art also remains popular with museum patrons. The [Guggenheim Museum](/wiki/Solomon_R._Guggenheim_Museum "Solomon R. Guggenheim Museum") in New York City held an exhibit, *Two Private Eyes*, in 1999, and in 2001 [Tate Modern](/wiki/Tate_Modern "Tate Modern") held an exhibition of Surrealist art that attracted over 170,000 visitors. In 2002 the [Met](/wiki/Metropolitan_Museum "Metropolitan Museum") in New York City held a show, *Desire Unbound*, and the [Centre Georges Pompidou](/wiki/Centre_Georges_Pompidou "Centre Georges Pompidou") in Paris a show called *La Révolution surréaliste*. In 2021–2022, The Metropolitan Museum of Art and Tate Modern co-organized the international survey exhibition *[Surrealism Beyond Borders](/wiki/Surrealism_Beyond_Borders "Surrealism Beyond Borders")*, bringing together examples produced across 45 or more countries over almost eight decades to reassess Surrealism’s transnational networks beyond a primarily Western European orientation.[[90]](#cite_note-MetSBBPress2021-90)

Surrealist groups and literary publications have continued to be active up to the present day, with groups such as the [Chicago Surrealist Group](/wiki/Chicago_Surrealist_Group "Chicago Surrealist Group"), the Leeds Surrealist Group, Peculiar Mormyrid, and [The Surrealist Group in Stockholm](/wiki/The_Surrealist_Group_in_Stockholm "The Surrealist Group in Stockholm"). [Jan Švankmajer](/wiki/Jan_%C5%A0vankmajer "Jan Švankmajer") of the Czech-Slovak Surrealists continues to make films and experiment with objects.

## Impact and influences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Surrealism&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Impact and influences")]

While Surrealism is typically associated with the arts, it has impacted many other fields. In this sense, Surrealism does not specifically refer only to self-identified "Surrealists", or those sanctioned by Breton, rather, it refers to a range of creative acts of revolt and efforts to liberate imagination.[[91]](#cite_note-91) In addition to Surrealist theory being grounded in the ideas of [Hegel](/wiki/Georg_Wilhelm_Friedrich_Hegel "Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel"), [Marx](/wiki/Karl_Marx "Karl Marx") and [Freud](/wiki/Sigmund_Freud "Sigmund Freud"), to its advocates its inherent dynamic is [dialectical](/wiki/Dialectic "Dialectic") thought.[[92]](#cite_note-92) Surrealist artists have also cited the [alchemists](/wiki/Alchemy "Alchemy"), [Dante](/wiki/Dante_Alighieri "Dante Alighieri"), [Hieronymus Bosch](/wiki/Hieronymus_Bosch "Hieronymus Bosch"),[[93]](#cite_note-thecityreview1-93)[[94]](#cite_note-94) the [Marquis de Sade](/wiki/Marquis_de_Sade "Marquis de Sade"),[[93]](#cite_note-thecityreview1-93) [Charles Fourier](/wiki/Charles_Fourier "Charles Fourier"), [Comte de Lautréamont](/wiki/Comte_de_Lautr%C3%A9amont "Comte de Lautréamont") and [Arthur Rimbaud](/wiki/Arthur_Rimbaud "Arthur Rimbaud") as influences.[[95]](#cite_note-95)[[96]](#cite_note-96)

### May 68

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Surrealism&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: May 68")]

Surrealists believe that non-Western cultures also provide a continued source of inspiration for Surrealist activity because some may induce a better balance between instrumental reason and imagination in flight than Western culture.[[97]](#cite_note-97)[[98]](#cite_note-98) Surrealism has had an identifiable impact on radical and revolutionary politics, both directly — as in some Surrealists joining or allying themselves with radical political groups, movements and parties — and indirectly — through the way in which Surrealists emphasize the intimate link between freeing imagination and the mind, and liberation from repressive and archaic social structures. This was especially visible in the [New Left](/wiki/New_Left "New Left") of the 1960s and 1970s and the [French revolt of May 1968](/wiki/May_1968_in_France "May 1968 in France"), whose slogan "All power to the imagination" quoted by [The Situationists](/wiki/The_Situationists "The Situationists") and [Enragés](/wiki/Enrag%C3%A9s#Other_groups "Enragés")[[99]](#cite_note-99) from the originally Marxist "*Rêvé*-lutionary" theory and praxis of Breton's French Surrealist group.[[100]](#cite_note-100)

### Postmodernism and popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Surrealism&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Postmodernism and popular culture")]

Many significant literary movements in the later half of the 20th century were directly or indirectly influenced by Surrealism. This period is known as the [Postmodern](/wiki/Postmodern "Postmodern") era; though there is no widely agreed upon central definition of [Postmodernism](/wiki/Postmodernism "Postmodernism"), many themes and techniques commonly identified as Postmodern are nearly identical to Surrealism.

First Papers of Surrealism presented the fathers of surrealism in an exhibition that represented the leading monumental step of the avant-gardes towards installation art.[[101]](#cite_note-101) Many writers from and associated with the [Beat Generation](/wiki/Beat_Generation "Beat Generation") were influenced greatly by Surrealists. [Philip Lamantia](/wiki/Philip_Lamantia "Philip Lamantia")[[102]](#cite_note-102) and [Ted Joans](/wiki/Ted_Joans "Ted Joans")[[103]](#cite_note-103) are often categorized as both Beat and Surrealist writers. Many other Beat writers show significant evidence of Surrealist influence. A few examples include [Bob Kaufman](/wiki/Bob_Kaufman "Bob Kaufman"),[[104]](#cite_note-104)[[105]](#cite_note-105) [Gregory Corso](/wiki/Gregory_Corso "Gregory Corso"),[[106]](#cite_note-106) [Allen Ginsberg](/wiki/Allen_Ginsberg "Allen Ginsberg"),[[107]](#cite_note-107) and [Lawrence Ferlinghetti](/wiki/Lawrence_Ferlinghetti "Lawrence Ferlinghetti").[[108]](#cite_note-108) Artaud in particular was very influential to many of the Beats, but especially Ginsberg and [Carl Solomon](/wiki/Carl_Solomon "Carl Solomon").[[109]](#cite_note-109) Ginsberg cites Artaud's "Van Gogh – The Man Suicided by Society" as a direct influence on "[Howl](/wiki/Howl_(poem) "Howl (poem)")",[[110]](#cite_note-110) along with Apollinaire's "Zone",[[111]](#cite_note-111) García Lorca's "Ode to Walt Whitman",[[112]](#cite_note-112) and Schwitters' "Priimiititiii".[[113]](#cite_note-113) The structure of Breton's "Free Union" had a significant influence on Ginsberg's "Kaddish".[[114]](#cite_note-114) In Paris, Ginsberg and Corso met their heroes Tristan Tzara, Marcel Duchamp, Man Ray, and Benjamin Péret, and to show their admiration Ginsberg kissed Duchamp's feet and Corso cut off Duchamp's tie.[[115]](#cite_note-115)

[William S. Burroughs](/wiki/William_S._Burroughs "William S. Burroughs"), a core member of the Beat Generation and a postmodern novelist, developed the [cut-up technique](/wiki/Cut-up_technique "Cut-up technique") with former surrealist [Brion Gysin](/wiki/Brion_Gysin "Brion Gysin")—in which chance is used to dictate the composition of a text from words cut out of other sources—referring to it as the "Surrealist Lark" and recognizing its debt to the techniques of Tristan Tzara.[[116]](#cite_note-116)

Postmodern novelist [Thomas Pynchon](/wiki/Thomas_Pynchon "Thomas Pynchon"), who was also influenced by Beat fiction, experimented since the 1960s with the surrealist idea of startling juxtapositions; commenting on the "necessity of managing this procedure with some degree of care and skill", he added that "any old combination of details will not do. Spike Jones Jr., whose father's orchestral recordings had a deep and indelible effect on me as a child, said once in an interview, 'One of the things that people don't realize about Dad's kind of music is, when you replace a C-sharp with a gunshot, it has to be a C-sharp gunshot or it sounds awful.'"[[23]](#cite_note-PynchonSurralism-23)

Many other postmodern fiction writers have been directly influenced by Surrealism. [Paul Auster](/wiki/Paul_Auster "Paul Auster"), for example, has translated Surrealist poetry and said the Surrealists were "a real discovery" for him.[[117]](#cite_note-117) [Salman Rushdie](/wiki/Salman_Rushdie "Salman Rushdie"), when called a Magical Realist, said he saw his work instead "allied to surrealism".[[118]](#cite_note-118)[[119]](#cite_note-119) [David Lynch](/wiki/David_Lynch "David Lynch") regarded as a surrealist filmmaker being quoted, "David Lynch has once again risen to the spotlight as a champion of surrealism,"[[120]](#cite_note-120) in regard to his show *[Twin Peaks](/wiki/Twin_Peaks "Twin Peaks")*. For the work of other postmodernists, such as [Donald Barthelme](/wiki/Donald_Barthelme "Donald Barthelme")[[121]](#cite_note-121) and [Robert Coover](/wiki/Robert_Coover "Robert Coover"),[[122]](#cite_note-122) a broad comparison to Surrealism is common.

[Magic realism](/wiki/Magic_realism "Magic realism"), a popular technique among novelists of the latter half of the 20th century especially among Latin American writers, has some obvious similarities to Surrealism with its juxtaposition of the normal and the dream-like, as in the work of [Gabriel García Márquez](/wiki/Gabriel_Garc%C3%ADa_M%C3%A1rquez "Gabriel García Márquez").[[123]](#cite_note-123) [Carlos Fuentes](/wiki/Carlos_Fuentes "Carlos Fuentes") was inspired by the revolutionary voice in Surrealist poetry and points to inspiration Breton and Artaud found in Fuentes' homeland, Mexico.[[124]](#cite_note-124) Though Surrealism was a direct influence on Magic Realism in its early stages, many Magic Realist writers and critics, such as Amaryll Chanady[[125]](#cite_note-125) and S. P. Ganguly,[[126]](#cite_note-126) while acknowledging the similarities, cite the many differences obscured by the direct comparison of Magic Realism and Surrealism such as an interest in psychology and the artefacts of European culture they claim is not present in Magic Realism. A prominent example of a Magic Realist writer who points to Surrealism as an early influence is [Alejo Carpentier](/wiki/Alejo_Carpentier "Alejo Carpentier") who also later criticized Surrealism's delineation between real and unreal as not representing the true South American experience.[[127]](#cite_note-127)[[128]](#cite_note-128)

### Surrealist groups

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Surrealism&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Surrealist groups")]

See also: [Category:Surrealist groups](/wiki/Category:Surrealist_groups "Category:Surrealist groups")

Surrealist individuals and groups have carried on with Surrealism after the death of André Breton in 1966. The original Paris Surrealist Group was disbanded by member Jean Schuster in 1969, but another Parisian surrealist group was later formed. The current Surrealist Group of Paris has recently published the first issue of their new journal, *Alcheringa*. The Group of Czech-Slovak Surrealists never disbanded, and continue to publish their journal *Analogon*, which now spans almost 100 volumes.

### Surrealism and the theatre

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Surrealism&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Surrealism and the theatre")]

Surrealist theatre and Artaud's "Theatre of Cruelty" were inspirational to many within the group of playwrights that the critic Martin Esslin called the "[Theatre of the Absurd](/wiki/Theatre_of_the_Absurd "Theatre of the Absurd")" (in his 1963 book of the same name). Though not an organized movement, Esslin grouped these playwrights together based on some similarities of theme and technique; Esslin argues that these similarities may be traced to an influence from the Surrealists. [Eugène Ionesco](/wiki/Eug%C3%A8ne_Ionesco "Eugène Ionesco") in particular was fond of Surrealism, claiming at one point that Breton was one of the most important thinkers in history.[[129]](#cite_note-129)[[130]](#cite_note-130) [Samuel Beckett](/wiki/Samuel_Beckett "Samuel Beckett") was also fond of Surrealists, even translating much of the poetry into English.[[131]](#cite_note-131)[[132]](#cite_note-132) Other notable playwrights whom Esslin groups under the term, for example [Arthur Adamov](/wiki/Arthur_Adamov "Arthur Adamov") and [Fernando Arrabal](/wiki/Fernando_Arrabal "Fernando Arrabal"), were at some point members of the Surrealist group.[[133]](#cite_note-133)[[134]](#cite_note-134)[[135]](#cite_note-135)

Alice Farley is an American-born artist who became active during the 1970s in San Francisco after training in dance at the California Institute of the Arts.[[136]](#cite_note-:0-136) Farley uses vivid and elaborate costuming that she describes as "the vehicles of transformation capable of making a character's thoughts visible".[[136]](#cite_note-:0-136) Often collaborating with musicians such as [Henry Threadgill](/wiki/Henry_Threadgill "Henry Threadgill"), Farley explores the role of improvisation in dance, bringing in an automatic aspect to the productions.[[137]](#cite_note-137) Farley has performed in a number of surrealist collaborations including the [World Surrealist Exhibition](/wiki/World_Surrealist_Exhibition "World Surrealist Exhibition") in Chicago in 1976.[[136]](#cite_note-:0-136)[[138]](#cite_note-138)

## Alleged precursors in older art

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Surrealism&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Alleged precursors in older art")]

Various much older artists are sometimes claimed as precursors of Surrealism. Foremost among these are [Hieronymus Bosch](/wiki/Hieronymus_Bosch "Hieronymus Bosch")[[139]](#cite_note-139) and [Giuseppe Arcimboldo](/wiki/Giuseppe_Arcimboldo "Giuseppe Arcimboldo"), whom Dalí called the "father of Surrealism."[[140]](#cite_note-140) Apart from their followers, other artists who may be mentioned in this context include [Joos de Momper](/wiki/Joos_de_Momper "Joos de Momper"), for some [anthropomorphic](/wiki/Anthropomorphic "Anthropomorphic") landscapes. Many critics feel these works belong to [fantastic art](/wiki/Fantastic_art "Fantastic art") rather than having a significant connection with Surrealism.[[141]](#cite_note-141)
